item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for creepy hidden bugs.

I'd give you another +1 if I could for making a scp about watching paint dry :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well the article is well written, I have a problem an insect like this could be considered a safe class, it would seem more like a Euclid/Keter due to the fact of how several colonies can be and probably aren't contained. Due to it's masking itself as pain, it would also make it more difficult to discover and contain the colonies.

Colonies of SCP-1450 found outside of containment are to be immediately purged, with either fire or pesticides based on location and quantity of SCP-1450

This right here would make me believe that it would be Euclid due to the fact that they find colonies outside of containment and SCP-1450 being able to be seen and interacted with by the general public.

Leaving it as a +1 anyway due to how insect pain is an extreme interesting concept, and the article is well written

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this idea, but I have a small nit to pick: latex paint doesn't actually contain any natural latex. It's made of acrylic or PVA or some other polymer. I could see them finding some polymer(s) in their paint-like excrement, but I don't think they would describe it as "latex-based" and leave out names of the the actual polymer(s) they found in it.

Just my two tiny cents! Otherwise the idea of paint that eats you is really quite unpleasant. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really good one! +1

I'd only put the addendum in a collapsible, and in a box.

example here because i don't remember it's precise name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are plenty of Safe scips that could kill people; the class is mostly about how much of a pain containment is. In this case it's just "keep them in the box, don't open the box, and fumigate any you find in the wild."

(The hypnotic effect seems to be limited to a flash-bang/seizure-inducing type affect, so they aren't going to be getting out of containment by mesmerizing people, either.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only issue I have with this skip is the classification.

Why is it marked Safe, when it's shown to have hypnotic effects and that it strips a human down to the bone. I would imagine this would fall into the Euclid category, as there is potential for a breach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems to be the critters that bugs me and my family recently. \:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel the test log doesn't add to the skip; it largely repeats what's been said in the description.

The rest of it works pretty well though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the pattern they stun with the same, always? Have any Humans been able to resist the "stun"?
…depending on the answers, the pattern itself may qualify as an SCP, or at least a subclassification as a Cognitohazard.
If they use different patterns, but stun everyone, that presents a whole new can of worms to look into. What do they use to determine "what pattern to use"?

Neat idea, creepy, very incomplete. No way the Foundation would have stopped here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to mention being able to stun people simply by changing colors is certainly anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read all the way to the end not understanding why this SCP was featured, and then I sat and thought about it. At first it seems like a pretty simple concept (bugs what kills you), but the smaller details like the ability to digest latex paint and the fact that there is no taxonomical description that applies to these bugs really got me. It seems to be a really subtle implication that these things are definitely not a natural occurrence, but either something artificial or from somewhere unearthly. Much more interesting upon second look! +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow I just got tingles. Total +1 from me. Very original idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Intensely adequate, I'd say. I'm not much one for getting creeped, and besides that there isn't that much, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehh… I'm unimpressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn potent nightmare fuel. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a bit redundant to name all the classifications that it doesn't have. If it doesn't have a genus, it doesn't have a species, and so on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am a little curious as to why this is Safe and not Euclid… it hunts people, and could exist anywhere, so full containment would be extremely difficult if not impossible…

I do like it though~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a couple of tonal issues, like "voracious" appetite, but I'm inclined to agree that this is a very good concept, executed well.

The fact that I've just spent a weekend painting helps, too. Now I can smell them…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh jesus +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what would be awesome? Making that into a gif that very slowly changes colors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture is absolutely marvelous once you get into the description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …I…probably should have checked taxonomy before I posted. CHANGED!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted at pretty much the exact same moment as Sorts just described.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn hippie bugs with your psychedelic colors! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1450 is a colony of insects of unknown genus and species.

I think the document should say the lowest level taxonomic group of the insect which is known. Insects are a class. After class come order, family, genus, and species. So if it's of a known family name the family. On the other hand, if even its order is unknown, say that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was staring at that picture for so long looking for the thing before I finished reading the description. I was all… is it the cabinet? Is this a cabinet bug?

Then I realized… they got me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Troy. Troy. Hey Troy.

You should bow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously bro this is getting stupid. Just start bowing already christ.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: YOU SHOULD BE BOWING RIGHT NOW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: BOW TROY. BOW TO ME AS YOUR -GOD-.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice work. Welcome back, Harley.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel it's justified in being an SCP for several reasons:

A. It can eat and shit latex paint. That's theoretically something that could be evolved, but consider we only started using latex paints in the last few decades…

B. It hunts people. It's specifically designed to inhabit urban areas and hunt humans. There's a word for that: Bloody dangerous. It's two words, and now my colon is clenching a little because there's a lot of abandoned buildings around here. (What have I done?)

C. It's got no identifiable genus. That means there's nothing to tie it to the rest of the naturally evolved species on earth. This thing appears to have just POPPED into existence.

Given all that, I feel like the foundation would be justifiably paranoid enough to start burning it out of existence while studying a small sample.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I may have skipped over something major in the article here by accident, but is SCP-1450 really the type of anomalous the foundation would go after? I mean nothing besides being unidentified is really THAT out of the norm, even then, it could just be near extinction or something, a lot of the traits of it can be found in real life animals, In fact something very similar to this has probably existed somewhere in the universe in reality.

But I'm just being picky here, the idea is odd and creepy enough for that nit-picky problem to not ruin my enjoyment of the article, this gets a +1 in my metaphorical book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very cool, and highly original. +1
